title: Recommendation System — System Design
description: Design a recommendation system that returns a ranked list of items for a user and context from a huge catalog, fast. The walkthrough starts with simple candidate generation, refines recall with embeddings and ANN, then adds ranking, features, filtering, feedback, and resilient serving.

nodes.label: Client / App
nodes.description: Requests recommendations, renders impressions, and emits interaction events.

nodes.label: Recommendation API
nodes.description: Owns the online request path: cache lookup, recall, ranking, filtering, logging, and fallbacks.

nodes.label: Experiment / Model Router
nodes.description: Selects the active experiment arm, ranking model version, feature set, and fallback policy for a request.

nodes.label: Candidate Generation Service
nodes.description: Fans out to multiple recall sources, merges candidates, dedups them, and caps the set before ranking.

nodes.label: ANN Vector Index
nodes.description: Approximate-nearest-neighbor index over item embeddings for fast similarity recall.

nodes.label: Trending / Popularity Store
nodes.description: Precomputed popular and trending item lists by locale, category, surface, and time window.

nodes.label: User Profile / History Store
nodes.description: Longer-lived user interests, follows, blocks, history summaries, and cold-start attributes.

nodes.label: Item Catalog / Metadata
nodes.description: Authoritative item metadata, availability, policy status, language, category, and content attributes.

nodes.label: Ranking Model Service
nodes.description: Scores candidate user-item-context tuples with the selected model and online features.

nodes.label: Online Feature Store
nodes.description: Low-latency online feature reads for user, item, context, and session entities.

nodes.label: Eligibility / Diversity Re-ranker
nodes.description: Applies policy, availability, already-seen suppression, deduping, freshness, diversity, and business constraints.

nodes.label: Recommendation Result Cache
nodes.description: Caches recent recommendation lists by user, surface, context, model arm, and freshness window.

nodes.label: Impression & Interaction Stream
nodes.description: Durable stream of impressions, clicks, hides, dwell time, conversions, and request context.

nodes.label: Real-time Feature Updater
nodes.description: Consumes fresh events to update session, recency, counters, and short-window features.

nodes.label: Training Data Lake
nodes.description: Stores raw events, impressions, labels, snapshots, features, and training/evaluation datasets.

nodes.label: Offline Feature Pipeline
nodes.description: Batch-computes features and writes training and serving materializations from shared definitions.

nodes.label: Training / Embedding Pipeline
nodes.description: Trains ranking models, evaluates them, and builds fresh user/item embeddings.

nodes.label: Model Registry / Artifact Store
nodes.description: Versioned model artifacts, embedding snapshots, metrics, approvals, and rollback metadata.

nodes.label: Metrics / Quality Monitoring
nodes.description: Latency, errors, feature freshness, model quality, drift, guardrails, and experiment metrics.

functional: Return a ranked list of items for a user + context such as home, related items, or notifications.
Personalize from user history, session context, item metadata, and learned embeddings.
Filter ineligible or already-seen items, then enforce freshness and diversity.
Log impressions and interactions so the system can learn from both shown items and user actions.
Support cold-start users and items with popularity, contextual, content-based, and exploration paths.
nonFunctional: Low serving latency, roughly p99 under 100-200 ms, despite a huge item catalog.
Scale recall over millions of items without scoring the full catalog per request.
Keep models, embeddings, and features fresh enough for the product surface.
Avoid train/serve skew by sharing feature definitions between training and online serving.
Degrade gracefully to cached, popular, or simple heuristic recommendations during model or feature outages.
Support safe model rollout through experiments, monitoring, and rollback.
Respect privacy and data governance: consent-aware feature use, retention windows, right-to-delete propagation across stores and training data, sensitive-category suppression, and regional policy.

capacity.label: Request scale
capacity.value: ~50M DAU x ~10 rec requests/day -> ~500M req/day, ~6k RPS avg, ~20k RPS peak
capacity.note: The whole funnel runs per request, so peak RPS, not catalog size, sets serving compute.

capacity.label: Catalog size
capacity.value: millions to tens of millions of items
capacity.note: Scoring the full catalog per request is not viable; recall must shrink it first.

capacity.label: Recall fan-out
capacity.value: ~4-6 sources, K~200 each -> ~300-1000 candidates after dedup
capacity.note: Simple sources plus ANN are merged and deduped before ranking. At 20k RPS that is ~100k candidate-source calls/sec.

capacity.label: Feature reads
capacity.value: ~500 candidates x ~50 features, batched -> ~10k feature values/request, ~200M values/sec at peak
capacity.note: Feature fetch is a batched key-value read, not per-candidate round trips; it dominates the ranking stage budget.

capacity.label: Latency budget (p99 ~150 ms)
capacity.value: cache ~5 / route ~5 / parallel recall ~30 / feature batch ~30 / scoring ~40 / filters ~15 / serialize ~10 / async log ~0
capacity.note: Stages run partly in parallel; the sum is a worst-case envelope. Impression logging is async so it never blocks the response.

capacity.label: Embedding / ANN memory
capacity.value: ~20M items x 128-d float32 ~10 GB raw; HNSW graph ~2-3x; IVF-PQ compresses to ~1-2 GB
capacity.note: Index choice is a recall-vs-memory trade-off; replicate per serving shard for throughput.

capacity.label: Event + training volume
capacity.value: ~500M served lists/day x ~20 impressions -> ~10B impression rows/day; retain raw ~30-90 days, aggregates longer
capacity.note: Impressions, not just clicks, dominate write and storage volume; retention windows are a governance decision.

capacity.label: Refresh cadence
capacity.value: ranker retrain hourly-daily; embeddings + ANN rebuild daily; online features seconds-minutes
capacity.note: Model, feature-set, and embedding versions must stay compatible across a rollout (see the experiment router).

api.description: Get ranked recommendations for a user in a context. Surface, locale, device, and session scope the request; debug returns per-item exposure detail for offline tooling.

Log an impression or interaction event for training, online features, monitoring, and evaluation. Events are appended asynchronously; an idempotency/event id deduplicates client retries and a client timestamp lets late events be ordered.

dataModel.name: recommendation_request
dataModel.note: One served list, including context and model/experiment metadata for debugging and evaluation.

dataModel.name: recommendation_exposure
dataModel.note: One row per shown item, keyed by (request_id, item_id). This is the exposure log; without per-item rank, score, source, version, and propensity, learning from logged recommendations (counterfactual / off-policy evaluation, contextual bandits) is impossible. Replaces the lossy served_item_ids array.

dataModel.name: interaction_event
dataModel.note: The feedback signal. Include impressions, not just clicks, so training data is not positive-only. event_id is the idempotency key for client retries; client_ts orders late-arriving events against shown_at.

dataModel.name: user_profile
dataModel.note: Long-lived interests and constraints used by simple recall and cold-start logic. Consent flags gate which signals may feed features/training; deletion_requested_at drives right-to-delete propagation across stores, the event log, and training data.

dataModel.name: item_catalog
dataModel.note: Authoritative metadata used for content recall and eligibility filtering.

dataModel.name: online_feature
dataModel.note: Materialized serving features keyed by entity and feature set version.

dataModel.name: model_version
dataModel.note: Versioned ranking model and embedding artifacts with rollout and evaluation metadata.

patterns.name: Candidate generation (recall)
patterns.what: Cheaply gather a few hundred plausible items from bounded sources before expensive ranking.
patterns.whenToUse: Huge catalogs where scoring everything is infeasible.

patterns.name: Embedding + ANN retrieval
patterns.what: Represent users/items as vectors; retrieve nearest items via an ANN index.
patterns.group: Refine Recall with Embeddings and ANN

patterns.name: Two-stage ranking
patterns.what: Bounded recall first, then an expensive ML ranking pass over the small candidate set.
patterns.whenToUse: Balancing relevance quality against serving latency.

patterns.name: Feature store (online/offline parity)
patterns.what: Serve features online with the same definitions used to train offline.
patterns.whenToUse: Avoiding train/serve skew in ML systems.

patterns.name: Feedback loop
patterns.what: Log impressions and interactions, then refresh features, embeddings, and ranking models from them.
patterns.whenToUse: Systems that should improve from user behavior.
patterns.group: The Feedback Loop: Events, Training, Models

patterns.name: Post-ranking filters (diversity/dedup)
patterns.what: Apply business rules, dedup, freshness, and diversity after ranking.
patterns.whenToUse: When raw model scores aren't the final list.

steps.title: 1. Start with Simple Candidate Generation
description: Do not start by scoring the whole catalog. First build a simple recall layer that cheaply returns a few hundred plausible items from sources that already exist: recent popularity, trending by surface/locale, the user's history, followed categories, and catalog metadata.

This version is deliberately understandable and debuggable. It can serve cold-start users with popularity/contextual lists and can personalize lightly from user profile/history without needing embeddings yet.

The candidate generation service fans out to these sources in parallel, merges and dedups item ids, and caps the set before ranking. It establishes the recall stage before adding more sophisticated retrieval.

view.caption: The recommendation API calls candidate generation, which draws a bounded set from the trending store, the user's history store, and the item catalog.

steps.decisionPrompt: Before introducing ML retrieval, what simple sources can produce useful candidates quickly?

concepts.definition: Cheaply retrieving a few hundred plausible items from multiple bounded sources, not the whole catalog.
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes the expensive ranking tractable by shrinking millions of items to a small candidate set.
concepts.example: Trending by locale + user history + category/catalog matches -> ~300 candidates.

concepts.definition: Serving reasonable recommendations when there is little or no user-specific history.
concepts.whyItMatters: A real recommender must work before embeddings and personalized signals are reliable.
concepts.example: New user on the home surface gets local trending and category-based items.

whyNow: Start with a simple, explainable recall layer; it proves the funnel shape and gives you a fallback path before advanced retrieval.

recap.after: A bounded set of candidates from popularity, profile/history, and catalog/content sources.
recap.newRisk: Simple sources miss semantically similar or collaborative items, so recall quality can plateau.

traps.trap: Building only personalized recall and forgetting anonymous / cold traffic.
traps.why: A large share of requests are logged-out, new, or low-history users; a history-only recall returns little or nothing for them.
traps.instead: Always keep popularity / trending / contextual sources in the fan-out so every request has a usable fallback.

flows.name: Simple multi-source recall
flows.note: The first version fans out to explainable sources and returns a bounded, deduped candidate set.

steps.title: 2. Refine Recall with Embeddings and ANN
description: The simple recall layer is useful, but it misses items that are behaviorally or semantically similar but not captured by popularity, category, or direct history rules. Add an embedding-based recall source as a refinement.
Represent users, sessions, and items as vectors. Candidate generation queries an ANN index to retrieve nearest item vectors quickly, then merges those ids with the simple sources from step 1.

ANN is not the whole recommender. It is one recall source inside the candidate-generation fan-out, and it is useful because it improves recall quality while still keeping the candidate set bounded before ranking.

view.caption: Candidate generation adds embedding recall from the ANN vector index alongside its trending, history, and item-catalog sources.

options.name: HNSW graph index (default)
options.description: A navigable small-world graph indexes item embeddings, traversing neighbor links to find approximate nearest vectors fast as one recall source in the fan-out.
pros: Excellent recall-vs-latency at the candidate-generation budget — fast nearest-neighbor lookups over item vectors.
Handles high-dimensional embeddings well and supports incremental inserts as embeddings refresh.
Widely supported in vector stores, so it integrates cleanly as one recall source in the fan-out.
cons: Graph indexes are memory-hungry; the whole index must largely fit in RAM.
Rebuild/refresh from newly trained embeddings is non-trivial at catalog scale.
Tuning ef/M parameters trades recall against latency and needs care.

view.caption: Candidate generation recalls nearest neighbors from an HNSW graph-based ANN index, plus the trending, history, and catalog sources.

options.name: IVF + product quantization (IVF-PQ)
options.description: Vectors are clustered into inverted lists and product-quantized into compact codes, so probing a few lists searches a huge catalog in little memory.
pros: Quantization compresses vectors so a huge catalog index fits in far less memory.
Inverted-list probing scales to billions of items with tunable nprobe.
Cheaper to host at very large catalog scale than an in-memory graph.
cons: Quantization is lossy, so recall is lower than HNSW at the same latency.
Needs periodic retraining of the coarse quantizer as the embedding space shifts.
Tail latency depends on how many lists are probed.

view.caption: Candidate generation recalls candidates from an IVF-PQ index using quantized embeddings, alongside the trending, history, and catalog sources.

options.name: LSH (locality-sensitive hashing)
options.description: Locality-sensitive hash families bucket similar vectors together, so a lookup just gathers candidates from the query's matching buckets.
pros: Hash buckets give very cheap, simple approximate lookups with low build cost.
Easy to shard and update incrementally as items arrive.
Good baseline when embedding dimensionality is modest.
cons: Generally lower recall than HNSW/IVF-PQ for the same memory budget.
Needs many hash tables to hit good recall, inflating memory.
Sensitive to hash-family/parameter choices; tuning is fiddly.

view.caption: Candidate generation retrieves items from LSH hash buckets, alongside the trending, history, and item-catalog recall sources.

steps.decisionPrompt: What does ANN add that popularity, categories, and direct user history cannot capture?

concepts.definition: Vector representations of users/items with approximate nearest-neighbor lookup.
concepts.whyItMatters: Improves recall with semantic and behavioral similarity while staying fast over a huge catalog.
concepts.example: user/session vector -> ANN -> 200 similar item vectors, merged with simple candidates.
concepts.group: Refine Recall with Embeddings and ANN

whyNow: After the basic recall layer is clear, ANN is easier to justify as a targeted quality refinement rather than magic at the start.

recap.after: A richer candidate set that includes semantic and behavioral nearest-neighbor items.
recap.newRisk: Candidates are still unordered by predicted engagement and need ranking.

traps.trap: Treating ANN as a black box and only watching latency.
traps.why: An index can be fast but low-recall; you can ship a regression without noticing because the funnel still returns items.
traps.instead: Track recall@K against an exact-search baseline alongside p99 latency, index memory, build/rebuild time, and fresh-item insertion behavior, and tune ef/nprobe to a recall target.

traps.trap: Letting embeddings and the index go stale.
traps.why: New and fast-changing items have no (or outdated) vectors, so ANN silently stops surfacing them and recall quality drifts.
traps.instead: Rebuild/refresh the index on a cadence, support incremental inserts for fresh items, and version embeddings so the ranker and index stay compatible.

flows.name: Add ANN as one recall source
flows.note: ANN augments, rather than replaces, the simple recall sources.

steps.title: 3. Ranking the Candidates
description: The candidate set is small enough to score with an expensive ML model. The ranker predicts engagement (e.g. probability of click/like) for each (user, item, context) and orders them — this is where personalization quality lives.

Two-stage design: cheap recall, including simple sources plus ANN refinement, then a heavy ranking model over hundreds of items. The model uses features about the user, item, and context (next step). The ranker loads a versioned model from the registry so models can be updated/rolled back.
Only the top-N after ranking are returned, so the costly scoring runs on a bounded candidate set, keeping serving within the latency budget.

view.caption: The recommendation API picks a model arm via the experiment router (governed by the model registry) and sends the candidate batch to the ranking service, which loads the artifact and fetches online features.

options.name: Gradient-boosted trees ranker (default)
options.description: A gradient-boosted tree ensemble scores each candidate over tabular user, item, and context features, loaded as a versioned artifact from the registry.
pros: Strong accuracy on tabular user/item/context features with little tuning.
Fast to train and to score a few hundred candidates within the latency budget.
Feature importances aid debugging; versioned artifacts load cleanly from the registry.
cons: Doesn't natively consume raw embeddings or sequences as well as a neural model.
Large ensembles can grow scoring latency on big candidate sets.
Capturing deep feature interactions needs manual feature engineering.

view.caption: The ranking service scores the candidate batch with a gradient-boosted-trees model loaded from the registry, using features from the online feature store.

options.name: Deep neural ranking model
options.description: A neural network scores candidates, consuming raw embeddings and sequence features directly and learning nonlinear feature crosses.
pros: Learns nonlinear feature crosses and consumes embeddings/sequence features directly.
Typically higher ceiling on engagement metrics than trees for rich signals.
Shares representation learning with the embedding/recall stack.
cons: Heavier serving: GPU/accelerator or careful optimization to meet the latency budget.
More training infrastructure, data, and tuning to reach and keep its advantage.
Less interpretable, complicating debugging and guardrail analysis.

view.caption: A deep neural ranker scores the candidate batch, loading its artifact from the model registry and pulling features from the online feature store.

options.name: Logistic regression / linear model
options.description: A linear/logistic model scores candidates as a weighted sum of engineered features — the cheapest model to serve.
pros: Extremely cheap to score and trivial to serve — lowest latency option.
Coefficients are fully interpretable; easy to reason about and audit.
A solid, robust baseline and good fallback when heavier models are down.
cons: Captures little interaction structure, so engagement lift is limited.
Needs heavy manual feature engineering (crosses, bucketing) to compete.
Plateaus well below trees/deep models on rich signals.

view.caption: A logistic/linear ranker scores candidates from weights in the model registry and features from the online feature store, chosen by the experiment router.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why a separate cheap recall stage and expensive ranking stage instead of one model?

concepts.definition: Cheap recall to shrink the set, then a heavy model to order the candidates.
concepts.whyItMatters: Delivers high-quality ranking within a tight latency budget over a huge catalog.
concepts.example: simple + ANN recall ~500 -> ranking model scores 500 -> return top 20.

whyNow: Ranking is where relevance quality comes from; the two-stage funnel is the canonical recsys architecture interviewers expect.

recap.before: Bounded but unordered candidates from simple and ANN recall.
recap.newRisk: The model needs features at serving time — fast, and consistent with how it was trained.

traps.trap: Trusting offline AUC/NDCG and shipping on it.
traps.why: Offline metrics are computed on logs the current model produced; they reward agreeing with past behavior and routinely fail to predict online lift.
traps.instead: Use offline metrics as a guardrail/filter, then confirm with an online A/B test on real engagement and guardrail metrics.

traps.trap: Training labels straight from clicks, ignoring exposure/position bias.
traps.why: Items shown higher get more clicks regardless of relevance, so the ranker learns to reproduce its own past ranking (a feedback loop).
traps.instead: Build labels from the exposure log (step 6) — include impressions and rank/propensity — and apply position-bias correction or rank features.

flows.name: Experiment-aware batch ranking
flows.note: Model routing is separated from ranking so experiments, gradual rollout, and rollback are controlled outside request business logic.

messages.label: resolve experiment arm and model id

steps.title: 4. Features and the Feature Store
description: The ranking model needs features: user attributes/history, item attributes/popularity, and context — fetched within the latency budget at serving time. A feature store provides low-latency online reads of these features keyed by user/item.

The critical correctness issue is train/serve parity: the features used to train the model offline must match those served online, or the model sees different inputs than it learned on (train/serve skew). The feature store enforces shared feature definitions across both.
Features come from two pipelines: offline batch (computed periodically, materialized to the store) and real-time (updated from the event stream for recency, e.g. 'items viewed in this session').

view.caption: The ranking service reads the online feature store, which is fed by a real-time updater consuming the interaction stream and an offline pipeline materializing batch features from the data lake.

options.name: Feature store with offline + real-time pipelines (default)
options.description: Shared feature definitions materialize through both batch and real-time pipelines into one online store that training and serving read.
pros: Shared feature definitions across training and serving directly prevent train/serve skew.
Online reads are low-latency and keyed by user/item, fitting the serving budget.
Batch and real-time pipelines both materialize into one store, so recency and history coexist.
cons: A whole feature-platform (store + two pipelines) to build and operate.
Real-time and batch paths can still diverge subtly if definitions aren't enforced.
Backfilling a new feature for training history is its own project.

view.caption: The online feature store is populated by both a real-time updater from the interaction stream and an offline pipeline from the data lake, and the ranker reads from it.

options.name: Compute features at request time in the service
options.description: The serving code derives features inline from raw sources on each request, with no separate feature store.
pros: Features are always as fresh as the request — no staleness from a materialization lag.
No separate feature store to operate; logic lives in the serving code.
Simple to start: read raw sources and derive features inline.
cons: Hard to guarantee training uses the exact same computation — high train/serve skew risk.
Per-request feature computation eats the latency budget and load on source systems.
Reusing features across models/teams becomes copy-paste.

view.caption: Instead of a feature store, the ranking service computes features inline at request time by reading user history, item attributes, and recent events.

options.name: Precompute-only (batch) feature store
options.description: Batch jobs precompute and materialize all features into the store, so online serving is a pure lookup with no real-time pipeline.
pros: All features materialized by batch jobs, so online serving is a pure fast lookup.
Strong train/serve parity since both read the same materialized snapshots.
No real-time pipeline to build or operate — simplest store.
cons: Misses in-session recency (e.g. 'items viewed this session'), hurting personalization.
Features are as stale as the last batch run, so reactions lag user behavior.
Cold or fast-changing items are poorly served until the next batch.

view.caption: The online feature store is filled only by the offline pipeline materializing batch features from the data lake; the ranker reads precomputed values with no real-time path.

steps.decisionPrompt: What goes wrong if the features at serving time differ from those used in training?

concepts.definition: A store serving the same feature definitions online (serving) and offline (training).
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents train/serve skew, the classic silent ML failure.
concepts.example: Same '7-day click count' feature computed for training and served at inference.

whyNow: Features make ranking work, and train/serve parity is the subtle ML-systems correctness point that separates a strong answer.

recap.newRisk: Raw model scores still aren't the final list — business rules, dedup, diversity apply.

traps.trap: Computing serving features differently from training features.
traps.why: The model receives inputs it never trained on; quality silently degrades.
traps.instead: Define features once in a feature store used by both training and serving.

traps.trap: Building training sets without point-in-time correctness.
traps.why: Joining a label to feature values computed after the event leaks future information; offline metrics look great and collapse online.
traps.instead: Materialize features as-of the request timestamp (point-in-time joins) and backfill new features over history the same way before training on them.

flows.name: Online and offline feature parity
flows.note: Fresh events update short-window online features, while batch jobs materialize the same feature definitions used for training.

steps.title: 5. Post-Ranking Filters and Diversity
description: The model's top scores aren't the final list. Apply post-ranking rules: remove ineligible items (out of stock, region-blocked, policy), drop already-seen/recently-shown items, and enforce freshness.

This stage is also where privacy and policy land: suppress sensitive categories, honor regional eligibility, and respect user controls (hides, blocks, opt-outs) before anything is shown. The item catalog's policy_status, sensitive_category, and region_eligibility drive these checks.

Add diversity: a pure score sort can return ten near-identical items; re-rank for variety (category/author spread) so the list feels good, not repetitive. Business boosts/demotes (promotions, quality) also apply here.
These filters run on the small ranked set, cheaply, and produce the final ordered top-N returned to the user.

view.caption: After the ranking service scores candidates, the recommendation API sends the top-N through an eligibility/diversity re-ranker that checks availability against the item catalog.

options.name: MMR re-rank for diversity (default)
options.description: Maximal Marginal Relevance greedily re-ranks the top set, each pick trading relevance against similarity to already-chosen items to break up near-duplicates.
pros: Maximal Marginal Relevance balances relevance against similarity to already-picked items, breaking up near-duplicates.
Runs cheaply on the small ranked set, within the post-ranking budget.
A single lambda knob trades diversity against pure relevance.
cons: Greedy selection isn't globally optimal for diversity.
Needs a meaningful item-similarity signal (e.g. embeddings) to compare against.
Tuning lambda per surface is an ongoing effort.

view.caption: The re-ranker applies MMR over the ranked top-N to diversify results, checking eligibility against the item catalog.

options.name: Rule-based category caps
options.description: Explicit rules cap how many items per category or author may appear in the top-N list.
pros: Simple, explainable rules: at most N items per category/author in the top-N.
Trivial to implement and to reason about with product/policy teams.
Deterministic results that are easy to QA.
cons: Blunt: caps can drop a genuinely best item just because its category is full.
Doesn't capture finer-grained similarity beyond the chosen buckets.
Rule sprawl as more diversity dimensions are added.

view.caption: A category-cap filter limits how many items per category appear in the ranked top-N, validating eligibility against the item catalog.

options.name: Determinantal point process (DPP)
options.description: A determinantal point process models relevance and similarity jointly to select a globally diverse set rather than picking greedily.
pros: Models relevance and diversity jointly and selects a globally varied set, not greedily.
Principled probabilistic objective for set diversity.
Can outperform MMR on diversity quality for the final list.
cons: More compute to build the kernel and sample/select — heavier than MMR or caps.
Requires a good item-similarity kernel, which must be maintained.
Harder to explain to product stakeholders than simple caps.

view.caption: A determinantal point process selects a diverse top-N from the ranked candidates, checking eligibility against the item catalog.

steps.decisionPrompt: The model returns 20 nearly-identical items. What do you do before showing them?
whyNow: Filtering/diversity is what turns raw scores into a usable list; it's a distinct, often-overlooked stage of the funnel.

concepts.definition: Adjusting the ranked list to avoid redundancy and improve variety/eligibility.
concepts.whyItMatters: Model-optimal-by-score can be a monotonous or non-compliant list.
concepts.example: MMR-style re-rank to spread categories; drop already-seen items.

recap.newRisk: Without learning from what users do, recommendations stagnate — need feedback.

traps.trap: Enforcing policy / privacy only at training time, not at serving time.
traps.why: A model can still rank a sensitive, region-blocked, or opted-out item highly; if filters don't catch it, it gets shown.
traps.instead: Apply eligibility, sensitive-category suppression, regional rules, and user opt-outs as a hard post-ranking gate the model cannot override.

traps.trap: Over-diversifying or over-filtering into a filter bubble.
traps.why: Aggressive caps and same-as-before suppression can strip the genuinely best items or trap users in a narrow slice of the catalog.
traps.instead: Tune diversity per surface, keep an exploration slot, and monitor catalog coverage as a guardrail metric.

flows.name: Eligibility and diversity pass
flows.note: Raw model scores are not the final product list; the final pass enforces product constraints and user experience quality.

steps.title: 6. The Feedback Loop: Events, Training, Models
description: Recommendations should improve from behavior. Log every interaction (impression, click, view, like) to an event stream — this is both the training label source and the input to fresh features.

Offline pipelines consume the events to retrain the ranking model and rebuild embeddings periodically, publishing new versions to the model registry; the ANN index is rebuilt from refreshed embeddings. Real-time features (session behavior) update continuously from the same stream.

Close the loop carefully: log impressions (what was shown), not just clicks, so training isn't biased by only seeing positives — and to enable counterfactual evaluation. The exposure log keeps per-item rank, score, candidate source, and model/feature/experiment versions; when exploration or a contextual bandit is in scope, also log the selection propensity so off-policy / counterfactual evaluation is sound.

Governance closes the loop too: consent flags gate which signals may train models, retention windows bound how long raw events live, and right-to-delete requests must propagate into the event log and training data, not just the serving stores.

view.caption: Client and API events flow into the interaction stream, which feeds the data lake for the training pipeline, the real-time feature updater, and quality monitoring, closing the loop back to models and embeddings.

options.name: Periodic batch retraining (default)
options.description: Offline pipelines retrain the ranker and rebuild embeddings on full logged data on a schedule, publishing versioned models to the registry.
pros: Offline pipelines retrain the ranker and rebuild embeddings on full logged data, giving stable, well-evaluated models.
New versions publish to the registry with metrics, enabling shadow/A-B rollout and rollback.
Logging impressions (not just clicks) supports unbiased training and counterfactual eval.
cons: Models lag reality between runs, so fresh trends take a cycle to learn.
Full retraining is compute-heavy and scheduled, not continuous.
Cold items wait for the next training/embedding rebuild to be well-served.

view.caption: The interaction stream lands raw logs in the data lake, where a periodic training pipeline builds models, publishes them to the registry, and refreshes ANN embeddings.

options.name: Online / incremental learning
options.description: The model updates continuously from the live event stream, adapting to fresh behavior within minutes rather than per batch cycle.
pros: The model updates continuously from the event stream, adapting to fresh behavior within minutes.
Reacts fast to trends and new items without waiting for a batch cycle.
Smaller, steadier compute than periodic full retrains.
cons: Far harder to evaluate, guardrail, and roll back a continuously-moving model.
Vulnerable to feedback loops and sudden distribution shifts (a viral event poisons the model).
Streaming training infrastructure is operationally complex.

view.caption: An online/incremental trainer consumes the interaction stream directly and publishes model increments to the registry, skipping the batch data lake.

options.name: Contextual bandit (explore/exploit)
options.description: A bandit policy chooses items to balance exploiting known-good ones against exploring uncertain ones, updating online from observed rewards.
pros: Explicitly balances exploiting known-good items with exploring uncertain ones, easing cold-start.
Updates policy from rewards online, learning which items to surface faster.
Reduces feedback bias by deliberately surfacing under-served items.
cons: Exploration spends impressions on weaker items, a short-term engagement cost.
Reward attribution and off-policy evaluation are subtle and error-prone.
More complex to reason about and tune than supervised retraining.

view.caption: A contextual bandit policy reads click/dwell rewards off the interaction stream and updates its policy in the model registry to balance exploration and exploitation.

steps.decisionPrompt: What should you log to train the model well — only clicks, or something more?

concepts.definition: Logging interactions to retrain models and refresh features that drive future recs.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets the system learn and adapt; logging impressions avoids positive-only bias.
concepts.example: Impressions + clicks -> training labels -> new model -> better recs -> more data.
concepts.group: The Feedback Loop: Events, Training, Models

whyNow: The feedback loop is what makes it a learning system; logging impressions (not just clicks) is the senior nuance.

recap.after: Models + features + embeddings continuously refreshed from feedback.
recap.newRisk: New users/items have no history (cold start); serving must stay fast and resilient.

traps.trap: Logging only clicks (positive-only) instead of impressions too.
traps.why: The model never sees what was shown-and-ignored, so it can't learn negatives and offline evaluation is hopelessly biased.
traps.instead: Log the full exposure list per request with rank/score/source, and treat shown-not-clicked as a signal.

traps.trap: Running exploration or a bandit without logging propensities.
traps.why: Off-policy / counterfactual evaluation needs P(item was shown); without it you can't correct for the policy that generated the logs.
traps.instead: Log the exploration/selection probability on each exposure row so doubly-robust / IPS estimators are valid.

traps.why: Recommending popular items makes them more popular, starving the long tail and amplifying bias over successive retrains.
traps.instead: Reserve exploration capacity, debias labels, and monitor catalog coverage / tail exposure as guardrails.

flows.name: Impressions to retraining
flows.note: The system logs what was shown as well as what users clicked, so labels and evaluation are not positive-only.

steps.title: 7. Serving: Latency, Cold Start, and Resilience
description: The whole funnel (recall -> rank -> filter) must fit a tight latency budget (p99 ~150 ms): give each stage its own deadline, parallelize candidate generators, bound candidate count, batch feature reads, and cache recent recommendations per user/context for repeat requests. The cache key must include the model arm and a feature-freshness bucket so an experiment or a refresh doesn't serve stale lists.

Degrade per dependency, not all-or-nothing. If one recall source is slow, proceed with partial recall from the sources that answered. If the feature store times out, score with last-known/default features or a lighter fallback model. If the ranker is down, serve cached or popularity lists. Isolate dependencies (separate pools/circuit breakers) so one slow component can't exhaust the request budget, and apply backpressure / load-shed to the cheapest path under overload.

Cold start: new users get popularity/contextual recs until they have history; new items get content-based features and exploration (surface them to gather feedback) so they're not invisible until they have interactions.

Version compatibility is a serving concern: the model version, feature-set version, and embedding/ANN version selected for a request must be compatible, or a rollout scores against mismatched features/embeddings. The experiment router pins a consistent (model, feature-set, embedding) tuple per arm, and rollout is gradual (shadow/A-B) with rollback via the registry.

view.caption: The recommendation API first checks the result cache, then runs the funnel of experiment router, candidate generation, ranking, and re-ranker, emitting latency and quality signals to monitoring.

steps.decisionPrompt: The ranking model service times out. What should the user see instead of an error?
whyNow: Closing on latency budget, cold start, and graceful degradation makes the funnel actually servable and robust in production.

recap.newRisk: Feedback bias and A/B rollout risk — bounded by impression logging, exploration, and gradual rollout.

bottlenecks.mitigation: Bound candidate count; cache; lighter fallback model under load.

bottlenecks.issue: Feature batch read is the long pole.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Batch reads, co-locate the store, cache hot keys, and set a feature-fetch deadline with default/last-known features on timeout.

bottlenecks.issue: One slow recall source stalls the whole fan-out.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Per-source timeout and partial recall: proceed with whoever answered rather than waiting on the slowest.

failureDrills.scenario: The ranking model service is unavailable.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Serve cached recs or a popularity/heuristic ranking so the user still gets a sensible list.
failureDrills.mitigation: Fallback ranking + result cache + graceful degradation.

failureDrills.scenario: The online feature store times out or returns stale values.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Score with last-known/default features or a lighter feature-light model, and flag the response as degraded rather than blocking.
failureDrills.mitigation: Feature-fetch deadline + default/last-known feature fallback + degraded-mode metric.

failureDrills.scenario: The ANN index is stale or lagging an embedding refresh.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Keep serving from simple recall sources and the previous index version; new items lean on content/exploration paths until the rebuild lands.
failureDrills.mitigation: Versioned embeddings/index, atomic index swap, and recall@K monitoring to catch a bad rebuild before promotion.

failureDrills.scenario: The event stream is down or backed up.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Serving continues; impression/interaction logging buffers and retries so the response path is never blocked on logging.
failureDrills.mitigation: Async append with local buffering + idempotent retry; alert on logging lag because it starves training and online features.

failureDrills.scenario: A bad model is rolled out (quality regression).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Guardrail metrics trip and the experiment router rolls back to the last good (model, feature-set, embedding) tuple.
failureDrills.mitigation: Shadow/A-B with guardrails, automatic rollback target in the registry, and version-compatibility pinning.

traps.trap: Caching recommendation lists without keying on model arm and feature freshness.
traps.why: An experiment arm or a feature/model refresh serves stale or wrong-arm lists, corrupting both UX and experiment attribution.
traps.instead: Include model arm + feature-freshness bucket in the cache key and invalidate on rollout.

traps.trap: Blocking the response on synchronous impression logging.
traps.why: Tying the request to the event stream couples tail latency and availability to the logging path.
traps.instead: Log impressions asynchronously (buffer + retry) off the critical path.

traps.trap: Rolling out a model against mismatched features or embeddings.
traps.why: A new ranker scored on an old feature set or stale embeddings silently degrades — train/serve skew at rollout time.
traps.instead: Pin and validate compatible model / feature-set / embedding versions per arm before promotion.

flows.name: Graceful serving path
flows.note: The serving path attempts the full funnel but can fall back to cache or popularity when dependencies are slow.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Recommendation Funnel
finalDesign.description: A recommendation request first checks a context-aware result cache (keyed by user/context, model arm, and feature freshness) and model/experiment routing. Candidate generation starts with simple bounded sources such as popularity, user profile/history, and catalog/content metadata, then refines recall with an embedding ANN source for semantic and behavioral similarity. The ranker scores the merged candidate batch with the selected model version and online features, while eligibility and diversity re-ranking use catalog metadata (including policy and sensitive-category controls) to produce the final top-N. The API logs both impressions and request context as a per-item exposure log (rank, score, source, versions, propensity), while the client logs interactions; those events feed a real-time feature updater, the training data lake, offline feature jobs, model training, embedding refresh, model registry rollout, and quality monitoring. The experiment router and model registry pin a compatible (model version, feature-set version, embedding/ANN version) tuple per arm so a rollout never scores against mismatched features or embeddings, with guardrail-driven rollback. This closes the feedback loop while keeping the serving path fast, observable, experimentable, cold-start-aware, governance-aware, and able to degrade to cache or popularity fallbacks.

view.caption: The recommendation API serves from cache or runs the full funnel (experiment router, candidate generation with ANN/trending/history/catalog recall, ranking with the feature store, and diversity re-ranking), while the interaction stream feeds training, feature, and monitoring pipelines that refresh models and embeddings.

flows.name: End-to-end online serving
flows.note: A single recommendation request moves through cache, experiment routing, multi-source recall, batch ranking, filtering, logging, and response.

flows.name: Feedback to model refresh
flows.note: Impressions and interactions become fresh features, training data, evaluated model versions, refreshed embeddings, and rollout decisions.

functional.how: User history/profile, embeddings, and online features in recall + ranking.
Request impression logs + client interactions feed real-time features, training data, and evaluation.
Simple popularity/contextual recall works before history exists; content features and exploration surface new items, and serving falls back to these paths when personalization is thin.

nonFunctional.how: Cache lookup, parallel bounded recall, batch ranking, and fast online features.
Offline retrain + real-time features from events.
Per-stage deadlines, partial recall, feature/ranker fallbacks, dependency isolation, experiment-aware rollback, result cache, and popularity fallback.

Consent flags gate feature/training use; sensitive-category and regional rules suppress items at filter time; retention windows bound raw events; right-to-delete propagates across stores, the event log, and training data.

say: Confirm ranked personalized recs over a huge catalog, served fast.
Pin the recall->rank funnel and low latency as central.
Note the feedback loop and train/serve parity as ML-systems concerns.
Start with simple bounded candidate generation from popularity, profile/history, and catalog sources.
Refine recall with embeddings and ANN as one additional source.
Then rank, filter, and log feedback.
Serving latency, cold start, and graceful fallback.
Tradeoffs: recall breadth vs latency, model freshness, exploration vs exploitation.
Mention A/B testing and counterfactual evaluation as extensions.

expectations: Uses simple recall sources and logs interactions.
May miss ANN refinement, feature parity, impression logging, and cold-start details.
Builds the simple recall -> ANN refinement -> ranking -> filtering funnel.
Uses a feature store with train/serve parity.
Explains feedback loops, impression logging, cold start, and graceful fallback.
Reasons about train/serve skew, logging bias, exploration, and counterfactual evaluation.
Designs offline/online pipelines, ANN refresh, model rollout, and observability.
Quantifies latency-budget tradeoffs and defines safe fallback behavior under dependency failure.

followUps: What exact features would you compute online vs offline, and how do you avoid train/serve skew?
What do you log for impressions, clicks, hides, dwell time, and request context so training is not biased?
How would you decide when to add ANN recall instead of relying on simple popularity/profile/catalog sources?
How would you balance exploration vs exploitation for new users and new items?
How would you A/B test a new model and roll it back safely?
How do you keep embeddings, the ANN index, and online features fresh without breaking serving latency?
What exposure metadata (rank, score, source, versions, propensity) would you log so counterfactual / off-policy evaluation is valid?
How do you split the p99 latency budget across cache, recall, feature fetch, scoring, and filtering — and what do you shed first under overload?
How do you handle consent, retention, and right-to-delete across the serving stores, event log, and training data?
How do you guarantee the model, feature-set, and embedding versions are compatible across a rollout?

links.groupDescription: Real systems that show the recall, ranking, feedback, and serving tradeoffs behind the interview design.
